faq:
Frequently Asked Questions

Can I sprinkle coffee grounds directly on my strawberry beds?

It is best not to. Thick layers of fresh grounds compact into a water-repellent mat that blocks moisture from reaching the roots, and the high nitrogen can tie up nutrients during fruiting. If you do use them as mulch, keep the layer under half an inch and never pile them up.

Do I need coffee grounds to lower my soil pH for strawberries?

Probably not. Strawberries like a pH of about 5.5 to 6.5, and most Santa Cruz soils already fall in that range, so extra acidification is usually unnecessary. Test your soil before assuming it needs adjusting.

What helps strawberries more than coffee grounds?

Focus on the fundamentals: consistent watering during fruit set, a layer of straw mulch to keep berries off the soil and prevent rot, and a balanced spring fertilizer. These do far more for your harvest than any coffee ground treatment.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which blueberry varieties grow best in California?

Choose southern highbush varieties bred for fewer chill hours, such as Misty, Sunshine Blue, or O'Neal. These perform well in our mild-winter regions where northern types would struggle.

Why are containers or raised beds recommended for blueberries here?

Blueberries need an acidic soil pH of about 4.5 to 5.5, but most California soils measure around 7.0 or higher. Growing in containers or raised beds with an acidic potting mix lets you control the pH instead of fighting your native soil.

Do I need more than one blueberry plant?

Planting two southern highbush varieties encourages cross-pollination and better fruit set. Half-barrels filled with acidic mix work well, and the cool, foggy Santa Cruz summers actually reduce water stress on the plants.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I have to choose only natives or only Mediterranean plants?

No, and a hybrid approach often works best. Use California natives like coastal scrub and chaparral species as your structural backbone, then add Mediterranean plants such as lavender or rosemary for color and herbs.

How much water can each type save?

California natives can use 60 to 80 percent less water than a conventional lawn, while Mediterranean plants still achieve a 50 to 70 percent reduction. Both are strong choices for our dry summers once established.

Where can I find good native plants near Santa Cruz?

The UCSC Arboretum and Sierra Azul Nursery are excellent local sources for natives suited to our coastal conditions. Coast buckwheat is a forgiving starter plant that supports pollinators.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much sun do shade-tolerant vegetables actually need?

Most leafy greens and many herbs do well with about 3 to 4 hours of direct sun, and some root vegetables tolerate 4 to 5 hours. Afternoon shade can even help by slowing bolting and extending the harvest.

Which vegetables will not grow in partial shade?

Warm-season crops like tomatoes, peppers, squash, and corn need 6 to 8 hours of direct sun minimum. Reserve your sunniest corners for these and use shaded areas for greens and roots.

How do I figure out how much sun my garden gets?

Map your sun exposure by checking each area at 9 a.m., noon, and 3 p.m. on a clear day. In warmer inland spots like Scotts Valley and Watsonville, that midday and afternoon shade becomes especially valuable for cool-season crops.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will citrus peels kill the microbes in my compost?

No. Citrus peels contain limonene, which has mild antimicrobial properties, but in an active pile full of billions of microorganisms a few peels make no meaningful difference. A hot, active pile breaks the peels and oils down within weeks.

How should I add citrus peels to my compost?

Chop them into small pieces so they break down faster, and mix them in with other materials like leaves, cardboard, and food scraps rather than dumping them in one spot. This keeps the pile balanced and decomposing evenly.

Can I put citrus in a worm bin?

In small amounts, yes, though worms prefer other foods. Keep citrus to roughly 10 percent of the total scraps and add it gradually so the worms are not overwhelmed.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which produces more beans in a small space?

Pole beans, by a wide margin. They can yield two to three times as much as bush beans in the same ground area because they keep producing along their vertical growth for weeks.

When would I choose bush beans instead?

Bush beans mature quickly, in roughly 50 days, and give a concentrated harvest that is handy for freezing. They are ideal for succession planting every few weeks or for filling gaps between other crops, and they need no trellis.

When should I plant pole beans in Santa Cruz County?

Plant after the last frost, around May, in your sunniest bed. Varieties like Kentucky Wonder or Blue Lake will keep producing into September given our long growing season, especially with regular picking.

Frequently Asked Questions

What basic parts do I need for a raised-bed drip system?

You need a timer at the hose bib, a backflow preventer, a filter, and a pressure regulator (set around 25 psi), plus half-inch mainline tubing and quarter-inch emitter lines. Half-gallon-per-hour emitters spaced along the bed work well for vegetables.

How often should I run drip irrigation in summer?

Most beds need about 20 to 30 minutes every other day in summer, ideally before 7 a.m. to limit evaporation. Sandy soils like those in the Pajaro Valley may need daily watering, while moisture-retentive Westside soils can go longer between cycles.

Why does my drip system clog or fail?

The most common cause is not walking the lines before relying on the system. Run a test before planting to catch clogged emitters, loose connections, and kinks, then check the lines monthly through the season.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are butterflies and moths actually harmful in the garden?

The adults are mostly harmless pollinators, but the trouble comes from their caterpillar stage. Larvae of species like the cabbage white, tomato hornworm, and codling moth can do real damage to crops.

What is the most effective way to protect my brassicas?

Floating row covers are the single most effective and least toxic option, because they physically block egg-laying adults while still letting in light and water. Put them on before the cabbage whites start flying.

Can I support butterflies without sacrificing my vegetables?

Yes. Set aside non-crop areas planted with natives like milkweed and coyote brush so butterflies have habitat, while you protect your vegetable beds with row covers or targeted controls.

Frequently Asked Questions

When should I hand-pick caterpillars instead of using Bt?

Hand-picking is ideal for a small garden with just a few brassica plants and light pressure. A five-minute daily check of leaf undersides is free, completely safe, and helps you learn to tell pests from beneficial insects.

Is Bt safe for bees and other beneficial insects?

Yes. Bt targets only caterpillars and is considered safe for bees, other beneficials, birds, pets, and people, and it is approved for organic gardening. It typically kills caterpillars within two to three days after they ingest it.

What works best in Santa Cruz during peak season?

For a typical bed of 6 to 12 brassicas from March through June, combine morning inspections with Bt applications every 7 to 10 days. Our overcast mornings actually extend Bt's effectiveness compared with sunnier regions, since sunlight breaks it down.

Frequently Asked Questions

What soil temperature do tomatoes need before transplanting?

Aim for soil that is consistently 60 degrees or warmer at a 4-inch depth, with nighttime air temperatures above 50 degrees. Soil temperature matters more than the calendar, so check it weekly with a probe thermometer.

Does the timing differ across Santa Cruz County?

Yes. Warmer microclimates like Scotts Valley, Watsonville, and the Pajaro Valley usually warm up by late April, while cooler coastal areas such as the Westside and Aptos often need to wait until mid-May.

Can I plant tomatoes earlier than that?

In warmer zones you can push to early April if you use protective structures like a Wall O' Water or row covers to keep the seedlings warm. Always harden off seedlings for 7 to 10 days first.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do purchased ladybugs leave my garden?

Most store-bought ladybugs are wild-collected during their migration season and retain a strong instinct to disperse. Research has found that 80 to 95 percent fly off within the first two days of release.

Are there downsides to releasing bought ladybugs?

Yes. Mass-collected ladybugs can carry parasitic fungi and mites that harm native populations, and overcollection threatens wild aggregations in California's foothills.

What attracts ladybugs and other beneficials to stay?

Plant nectar-rich flowers such as sweet alyssum, yarrow, coyote brush, and California buckwheat near your vegetable beds, and avoid broad-spectrum pesticides. Our mild climate supports native beneficial insects nearly year-round, so they will come and stay on their own.

Frequently Asked Questions

What can I still plant in Santa Cruz in May?

May is your last reliable window to transplant tomatoes, peppers, and eggplant, and a good time to direct-sow summer squash, cucumbers, melons, and beans. You can also plant basil and other heat-tolerant herbs.

How should I water as the dry season begins?

Switch to deep, infrequent watering, generally two to three times per week for most vegetables, to encourage deep root growth. Set your irrigation correctly now, because one missed emitter in May can mean a dead plant by July.

Which pests should I watch for this month?

Keep an eye out for tomato hornworms (hand-pick them), spider mites (a strong water spray disrupts colonies), and squash bugs (remove their egg clusters). Protect beneficial insects like parasitic wasps while you do.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I tell blossom end rot from sunscald?

Blossom end rot shows up as a dark, sunken patch on the bottom of the fruit, while sunscald appears as a pale, papery, bleached area on the sun-facing side. Their locations on the fruit are the easiest giveaway.

Is blossom end rot caused by a calcium deficiency in my soil?

Usually not. It comes from inconsistent watering that prevents the plant from moving calcium to the developing fruit, so cycles of drought followed by heavy watering trigger it. Steady moisture, helped by mulch and drip irrigation, is the fix.

Which problem is more common in Santa Cruz?

Blossom end rot is the more common issue locally because of watering inconsistencies. Sunscald mostly affects hotter inland areas like Watsonville, since coastal fog cover shields most coastal gardens from intense sun.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a good basic soil mix for raised beds?

Use roughly equal parts topsoil, compost, and a coarse material like aged bark or perlite. This blend drains well, holds moisture, and supplies the nutrients vegetables need.

Can I just fill my beds with bagged garden soil?

It is best to avoid bagged garden soil, which is too heavy and compacts in raised beds. Also use mushroom compost only as part of the mix rather than on its own, since it tends to be alkaline and salt-heavy.

Do I need to add to the soil each year?

Yes. Soil settles and breaks down over time, so top-dress with 2 to 3 inches of compost annually. Local bulk sources like Grab-N-Grow in Scotts Valley or the county compost facility cost far less than bagged products.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do beer traps actually reduce slug damage?

Only a little. Studies show beer traps cut damage by less than 30 percent, compared with over 80 percent for iron phosphate baits, because catching slugs and actually controlling their population are two different things.

Can beer traps make a slug problem worse?

They can. Traps attract slugs from only about three feet away, which may draw in more slugs than you started with, and they also catch beneficial ground beetles that eat slugs.

What works better than beer traps?

Iron phosphate bait such as Sluggo is OMRI-certified organic, safe for pets and wildlife, and stays effective for up to two weeks even in rain. Pair it with morning watering and removing slug hiding spots like boards and debris.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why are beneficial insects better than pesticides for aphids?

Beneficials like ladybugs, lacewings, and syrphid flies establish self-sustaining populations that keep aphids in check season after season at no recurring cost. Pesticides give a quick knockdown but kill those same predators, which often makes future infestations worse.

How do I attract beneficial insects to my garden?

Plant insectary flowers such as sweet alyssum, yarrow, dill, fennel, and California poppies near your vegetable beds. These provide the nectar and pollen that draw in and hold predator insects.

Does biological control work well in Santa Cruz?

Yes, especially here. Our mild climate keeps beneficial insects active nearly year-round, which makes biological control more effective than in most of the country. Accepting a few aphids is part of keeping that natural balance.
